items.question_text: How soon must you act if a position report affecting separation is not received?

choices.text: No later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.
No later than 10 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.
Only after the aircraft misses the next required report as well.
Only when the pilot later calls to say the report was omitted.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-2, if a position report affecting separation is not received, action is taken no later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.

items.question_text: What is prohibited regarding duplicate position reports?

choices.text: Requiring the aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility.
Requesting any position report that was not filed in the flight plan.
Accepting position reports relayed through another aircraft.
Using position reports when radar is unavailable.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-3, an aircraft must not be required to make the same position report to more than one facility.

items.question_text: Who applies the adjacent-airport wake-turbulence separation?

choices.text: The ATC facility having control jurisdiction at the adjacent airports.
The pilot following the wake-producing aircraft.
Only the tower at the airport where the heavier aircraft is landing.
The first facility that receives the aircraft's departure message.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-4, the ATC facility with control jurisdiction at adjacent airports applies the wake-turbulence separation when the described crossing-flight-path conditions exist.

items.question_text: When do these arrival wake-turbulence minima apply?

choices.text: When IFR aircraft land behind other arriving aircraft to the same runway, a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet, or a crossing runway if the projected flight paths will cross.
Only when both aircraft are using entirely different airports in the same metro area.
Only when the following aircraft requests visual separation from the traffic ahead.
Only when the lead aircraft is executing a missed approach.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-5, the arrival minima apply to IFR aircraft landing behind other arriving aircraft in the listed same-runway, close-parallel, or crossing-runway situations.

items.question_text: When may the diverging-course departure minima be used?

choices.text: When aircraft will fly courses diverging by 45 degrees or more after departing the same or adjacent airports.
Whenever the aircraft are assigned different altitudes after departure, regardless of course.
Only when the divergence is at least 90 degrees and both aircraft are turbine-powered.
Only after radar identification has been established on both aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 6-2-1, the paragraph applies when aircraft depart the same or adjacent airports and will fly courses diverging by 45 degrees or more.

items.question_text: What is one authorized same-course minimum when the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft?

choices.text: Use 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft.
Use 1 minute until the following aircraft is airborne.
Use 10 minutes in every case, regardless of equipment.
Use 5 miles even if the aircraft are not eligible for the distance-based method.

items.explanation: Per 6-2-2, one option is 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft.

items.question_text: What traffic pair is separated by this rule?

choices.text: A departing aircraft and an arriving aircraft making an instrument approach to the same airport.
Two aircraft both established inbound on the same final approach course.
Two departing aircraft from adjacent airports on diverging courses.
A VFR departure and a VFR arrival to unrelated airports.

items.explanation: Per 6-3-1, the paragraph addresses separating a departure from an arrival making an instrument approach to the same airport.

items.question_text: What speed advantage supports the first listed distance-based minimum for aircraft on the same, converging, or crossing courses?

choices.text: The leading aircraft maintains a speed at least 44 knots faster than the following aircraft.
The following aircraft maintains a speed at least 22 knots faster than the leading aircraft.
The leading aircraft is any amount faster, regardless of the difference.
The aircraft are within 44 miles of the same NAVAID.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-2, the first listed distance-based minimum uses a leading aircraft speed advantage of at least 44 knots.

items.question_text: Which report is one way to discontinue opposite-course vertical separation?

choices.text: Both aircraft report passing the same intersection or waypoint and are at least 3 minutes apart.
Either aircraft reports it expects to pass the other traffic shortly.
The leading aircraft reports level at cruise altitude while the other remains opposite direction.
The controller observes matching ground speeds on the flight strips.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-3, one listed condition is that both aircraft report passing the same intersection or waypoint and be at least 3 minutes apart.

items.question_text: Before authorizing pilot-maintained longitudinal separation on the same course, what must be true?

choices.text: The pilots are in direct radio communication with each other and concur.
The following aircraft must already be at least 20 miles behind the lead aircraft.
The aircraft must be on opposite courses at different altitudes.
The lead pilot must cancel IFR before the other pilot assumes separation.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-4, the pilot-maintained longitudinal separation may be authorized when pilots on the same course are in direct radio communication and concur.

items.question_text: What must you tell an RNAV aircraft when DME separation is being applied along a VOR airway or route?

choices.text: Use DME distances.
Use ATD distances.
Use slant-range corrected RNAV distances only.
Use airway centerline mileage instead of DME mileage.

items.explanation: Per 6-4-5, when DME separation is applied to an RNAV aircraft along VOR airways or routes, the pilot is advised to use DME distances.

items.question_text: Which is one approved lateral-separation method?

choices.text: Clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose widths or protected airspace do not overlap.
Clear both aircraft onto the same protected route and rely on pilot visual separation.
Use any intersecting holding fixes as long as the pilots agree on spacing.
Assume separation exists whenever the aircraft report different headings.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-1, one approved method is to clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose widths or protected airspace do not overlap.

items.question_text: When may separation be considered to exist on radials of the same NAVAID?

choices.text: When the radials diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the airspace protected for the other aircraft.
When the radials diverge by at least 5 degrees and both aircraft are within 15 miles of the NAVAID.
Only after both aircraft have crossed the 51-mile point from the NAVAID.
Only if both aircraft are operating on published airways rather than random routes.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-2, separation exists when the described radials diverge by at least 15 degrees and either aircraft is clear of the airspace protected for the other.

items.question_text: What minimum applies between different DME arcs about a NAVAID at 35 miles or less from that NAVAID?

choices.text: 10 miles.
5 miles.
15 miles.
20 miles.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-3, different arcs about a NAVAID at 35 miles or less use 10 miles.

items.question_text: For direct courses and course changes of 15 degrees or less, what protected width is used on each side of the route out to 51 miles from the NAVAID?

choices.text: 4 miles on each side of the route.
2 miles on each side of the route.
6 miles on each side of the route.
10 miles on each side of the route from the NAVAID outward.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-4, direct courses and course changes of 15 degrees or less use 4 miles on each side of the route out to 51 miles from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: For an RNAV route that is 8 miles or less in width, how much non-overlap is required before lateral separation exists?

choices.text: 4 miles.
2 miles.
8 miles.
10 miles.

items.explanation: Per 6-5-5, when the route is 8 miles or less in width, 4 miles of non-overlap is required.

items.question_text: Under the basic vertical-separation rule, when may you assign an altitude to another aircraft?

choices.text: After the aircraft previously at that altitude has reported leaving it.
As soon as the previous aircraft is expected to begin climbing or descending.
Only after the previous aircraft reports reaching the next assigned altitude.
Only after radar confirms 5 miles of spacing between the aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-1, the next aircraft may be assigned the altitude after the aircraft previously at that altitude reports leaving it.

items.question_text: Which is one case where you must wait until the previous aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum before assigning the altitude?

choices.text: Severe turbulence is reported.
The aircraft is making a routine climb with no special conditions.
The aircraft is level at cruise altitude and no exceptions apply.
The pilot requests a lower altitude on initial contact.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-2, one listed exception is severe turbulence, which requires the more restrictive reassignment rule.

items.question_text: Which aircraft may be authorized to maintain vertical separation when pilots concur and are in direct radio communication during climb or descent?

choices.text: The lower aircraft if climbing, or the upper aircraft if descending.
Only the leading aircraft, regardless of whether it is climbing or descending.
Only the lower aircraft, regardless of whether it is climbing or descending.
Either aircraft, but only after canceling IFR.

items.explanation: Per 6-6-3, the lower aircraft may be authorized if climbing, or the upper aircraft if descending, when the pilots concur and are in direct radio communication.

items.question_text: At what kind of airport may timed approaches be used?

choices.text: An airport served by a tower, provided the listed conditions are met.
Any airport, even without a tower, as long as pilots request them.
Only airports with precision approaches and no missed-approach procedures.
Only airports where all traffic is radar vectored to final.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-1, timed approaches may be used at airports served by a tower when the listed conditions are met.

items.question_text: When should an established timed-approach sequence be interrupted?

choices.text: When necessary to allow an aircraft to execute a different type of approach.
Whenever the next aircraft reports the outer marker inbound.
Whenever radar contact is established with the following aircraft.
Whenever the tower requests a time check for convenience.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-3, interrupt the timed-approach sequence if necessary to allow an aircraft to execute a different type of approach.

items.situation: You have an established timed-approach sequence in progress, and the next aircraft now needs to fly a different type of approach than the sequence was built around.
items.para_context: The established timed-approach sequence is interrupted if necessary to allow an aircraft to execute a different type of approach.

choices.text: Interrupt the established timed-approach sequence as necessary to accommodate the different type of approach.
Keep the sequence unchanged because timed approaches cannot be interrupted once established.
Force the aircraft to continue with the original approach type regardless of operational need.
Cancel the entire arrival bank and close the approach control position immediately.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-3, the sequence may be interrupted when needed to let an aircraft execute a different type of approach.

items.instruction: Is this interval statement operationally correct?
items.question_text: The default minimum between successive timed approaches is 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval unless a listed exception applies.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 6-7-5, the basic interval is 2 minutes or 5 miles unless one of the listed exceptions applies.

items.question_text: Except as otherwise provided, what is the minimum between successive timed approaches?

choices.text: 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval.
1 minute or a 3-mile radar interval.
3 minutes or a 6-mile radar interval.
5 minutes only, with no radar-distance option.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-5, the basic minimum between successive timed approaches is 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval unless a listed exception applies.

items.question_text: When must you issue a time check before specifying a time to leave the approach fix inbound?

choices.text: Before specifying the time, unless the aircraft is vectored to the final approach course.
Only after the aircraft reports leaving the approach fix inbound.
Only when the aircraft is already established on final and not holding.
Never, because timed approaches assume the aircraft already has an accurate time source.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-6, a time check is issued before specifying the time to leave the approach fix inbound unless the aircraft is vectored to the final approach course.

items.situation: You are about to tell a non-vectored aircraft when to leave the approach fix inbound as part of a timed approach.
items.para_context: Issue a time check before specifying a time to leave the approach fix inbound unless the aircraft is vectored to the final approach course.

choices.text: Give the aircraft a time check before specifying the leave-fix time.
Skip the time check because the leave-fix time itself is sufficient.
Delay the time check until the aircraft reports crossing the final approach fix.
Replace the time check with a crossing altitude restriction.

items.explanation: Per 6-7-6, a time check precedes the specified leave-fix time unless the aircraft is being vectored to the final approach course.
